title: Anomaly-Induced Hybrid Bulk Electromagnetic Mode in Weyl Semimetals
authors: Subrahmanyam D, Suhas Gangadharaiah, E. G. Mishchenko
abstract: Collective modes provide direct fingerprints of quantum matter. We predict a previously unidentified hybrid bulk electromagnetic mode in Weyl semimetals arising from the interplay between the chiral anomaly and the orientation of its associated chiral magnetic response relative to the direction of the wave-vector. When the anomaly-induced chiral magnetic current has a component along the propagation direction, oscillations of valley imbalance hybridize with plasmonic charge oscillations, producing a linearly dispersing mode that undergoes avoided crossing with the bulk plasmon, producing a hybrid bulk excitation absent in ordinary metals. The hybrid mode provides a direct signature of Weyl semimetals and a probe of the chiral anomaly and its associated chiral magnetic effect, with observable features in electron energy-loss spectroscopy. Studying this interplay can uncover various optical and electronic properties of Weyl semimetals.
summary: This paper predicts a new type of bulk electromagnetic excitation in Weyl semimetals that occurs due to the chiral anomaly. This hybrid mode is distinct from known excitations and can be identified through its unique dispersion and avoided crossing with the bulk plasmon. The discovery offers a new way to probe the chiral magnetic effect using electron energy-loss spectroscopy.
main_problem: The identification and characterization of a previously unknown hybrid bulk electromagnetic mode in Weyl semimetals arising from the interplay between the chiral anomaly and the orientation of the chiral magnetic response.
main_result: The authors predict a linearly dispersing hybrid mode that undergoes an avoided crossing with the bulk plasmon when the chiral magnetic current has a component along the wave-vector, providing a direct signature of the chiral anomaly.
method: A semiclassical kinetic-Maxwell framework using the Boltzmann formalism and the continuity equation to solve coupled kinetic and Maxwell equations.
model_or_system: Doped 3D Weyl semimetals with broken time-reversal symmetry, characterized by a node-separation vector and a weak static external magnetic field.
key_observables: Loss function spectra L(omega, q), dispersion relations, and damping rates.
important_parameters: Orientation angle theta between the magnetic field and wave-vector, wave-vector q, frequency scale omega_anom, and the ratio of internode to intra-node scattering rates.
main_assumptions: Long-wavelength regime, collisionless limit, negligible Landau quantization, and neglecting interband effects due to low Fermi energies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the system; Figure 2 shows loss-function spectra L(omega, q) for different magnetic field configurations and angles, highlighting the presence or absence of the hybrid mode.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of anomaly-induced modes, describes the semiclassical kinetic-Maxwell model, analyzes the electromagnetic response in different magnetic field orientations (parallel vs. perpendicular), and concludes with the predicted experimental signatures in EELS.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic illustration of the anomaly-induced hybrid bulk electromagnetic mode in a Weyl semimetal in terms of breathing of the Fermi surfaces. Top left: in- phase breathing at opposite Weyl nodes, corresponding to the conventional plasmonic charge-density oscillation. Top right: antiphase breathing under E(t)·B0 ̸= 0, corre- sponding to a chiral charge-density oscillation. Bottom: for B0·q ̸= 0 and ωp ≃ωanom, coupling between the two oscillations yields a hybridized collective electromagnetic mode.

FIG. 2: Bulk electromagnetic modes (red triangles) over- laid on the loss-function spectra L(ω, q) for perpendicu- lar and parallel configurations. Peak in the loss function (blue) indicate the presence of collective modes, with their sharpness reflecting the degree of damping, while yellow regions correspond to negligible loss. Figure 2a shows B0 in the x–y plane at θ = 30◦with respect to ˆy, while Fig. 2b corresponds to B0 in the y–z plane at the same angle. Figures 2c and 2d (decoupled longitudinal sector) show B0 in the x–y plane at θ = 30◦and 0◦, re- spectively.
